Options 1 and 2 are tests of details, not analytical procedures.
Analytical procedures include the consideration of comparisons of the financial
information with. This may include comparisons with comparable information
for prior periods, with the client’s own budgets or forecasts, with expectations
of the auditor, with similar industry information, such industry averages or with
other companies of comparable size in the same industry

This scenario relates to four requirements.
It is 1 July 20X5.You are the audit supervisor of Glad & Co. and are planning
the audit of WeBuy Co for the year ending 30 September 20X5. WeBuy is an
online retailer of luxury bags and watches from leading brands sourced from all
over the world. Since this is the first time your firm is auditing WeBuy, the
planning meeting with finance director was detailed, notes have been
summarised below:
Planning meeting notes
Although company only sells through website, it has a network of three
warehouses in different cities to reduce shipping costs. The company’s
management has recently introduced a director’s bonus scheme to further
incentivize growth in profit. Directors’ bonuses were previously fixed however,
from this year, directors will be paid a bonus based on gross profit growth.
WeBuy’s payroll processing had always been outsourced to PayEm Ltd but
recently the management was not satisfied with the performance of the service
provider and therefore cancelled the contract in early June 20X5. PayEm had
been responsible for processing and making payroll details and maintenance
of employee records. Two of WeBuy’s accounts clerks were trained within a
week and given the task of maintenance of employee records and processing
of payroll in mid-June 20X5.
The initial analytical analysis of information yielded the following results.
Revenue growth is estimated to increase from 5% to 16% in a year while gross
margin will increase from 26% to 32%. The operating margin however is
predicted to drop from 19% to 12%.
WeBuy’s internal IT team developed its own mobile phone application for
$4.8m for selling luxury products through the application. The mobile
application will increase the ease with which costumers book orders and is
estimated to increase visitors on the platform. WeBuy has capitalised the
entire amount which includes $1.1m spent on legal fee for registering the
application with the regulator and training employees on how to use the
application for sales processing. The application is being tested and is
expected to be rolled out to customers soon.
WeBuy requires customers to make a deposit of 25% of the amount upon
order confirmation and the remaining amount is payable when goods are
delivered. WeBuy also offers its customers the facility to return product within
10 days if they are dissatisfied with the product or think it is a fake.
WeBuy has recently faced increasing criticism from the local tax authority for
alleged underpayment during the year amounting to $2.56m and investigations
by the tax authority are ongoing. The decision is not anticipated until after the
year end however WeBuy’s tax consultants have expressed concern that this
amount will probably have to be paid.
WeBuy offers a deal where slightly damaged luxury bags and watches are sold
at extremely discounted prices to clear inventory after the year end. This
happens during the first two days of October after year end. These deals are
hugely successful with sales volumes drastically increasing for those two days.

states that for identified risks of material misstatement at the assertion
level, the auditor must assess inherent risk by assessing the likelihood
and magnitude of misstatement. In doing so, the auditor needs to take
into account how, and the degree to which inherent risk factors affect the
susceptibility of relevant assertions to misstatement.

This question has three requirements.
You are an audit supervisor with Odie & Co, currently in charge of two clients,
Jon’s Junction Electrics Co and Lazlo Co.
It is 1 July 20X5. Jon’s Junction Electrics Co (JJE) is a manufacturer of
electrical equipment. The company’s year end is 30 September 20X5. You are
currently reviewing notes in relation to the internal controls in place at JJE.
Payroll
JEE has a workforce of 90 employees who monitor the machines. The
company manufactures 24 hours a day, seven days a week.
The company operates three shifts every day with employees working eight
hours each. They are required to clock in and out using an employee swipe
card, which identifies the employee number and links into the hours worked
report produced by the computerised payroll system. Employees are paid on
an hourly basis for each hour worked. The payroll department calculates on a
weekly basis the cash wages to be paid to the workforce, based on the hours
worked report multiplied by the hourly wage rate, with appropriate tax
deductions. As these calculations are generated by the payroll system with low
chances of errors, they are not checked. During the year the hourly wage was
increased by the Human Resources (HR) department and this was notified to
the payroll department at a team meeting.
Each Friday, the payroll department prepares the pay packets and physically
hands these out to the workforce, who operate the morning and late afternoon
shifts, upon production of identification. However, for the night shift workers,
the pay packets are given to the factory supervisor to distribute. If any night
shift employees are absent on pay day then the factory supervisor keeps these
wages and returns them to the payroll department on Monday.
Inventory count
JJE is planning to undertake a full inventory count at the year end of its raw
materials, work in progress and finished goods and you will be attending this
count.
The count will be undertaken by 10 teams of two counters from the warehouse
department with a senior official from JJE’s finance department providing
overall supervision. Each team of two will be allocated a number of aisles
within the warehouse to count and they will be provided with sequentially
numbered inventory sheets which contain information from the inventory
records ( product codes and quantities). The counters will move through each
allocated aisle counting the inventory and confirming that it agrees with the
inventory sheets. Where a difference is found, they will note this on the sheet.
The warehouse is large and some of the aisles have been rented out to third
parties with similar operations; these are scattered throughout the warehouse.
For completeness, the counters have been asked to count the inventory for all
aisles noting the third party inventories on separate blank inventory sheets,
and the finance department will make any necessary adjustments.
Some of JJE’s finished goods are high in value and are stored in a locked area
of the warehouse and all the counting teams will be given the code to access
this area. There will be no despatches of inventory during the count and it is
not anticipated that there will be any deliveries from suppliers.
(a) Identify and explain THREE DEFICIENCIES in JJE’s PAYROLL system
and provide a recommendation to address each of these deficiencies.
(6 marks)
(b) In respect of the INVENTORY COUNT procedures for JJE:
(i) Identify and explain THREE deficiencies;
(ii) Recommend a control to address each of these deficiencies; and
(iii) Describe a TEST OF CONTROL the external auditors would perform
to assess if each of these controls, if implemented, is operating
effectively. (9 marks)
(c) Discuss the benefits to Lazlo Co of forming an audit committee.
(5 marks)
(20 marks)
This scenario relates to three requirements.
It is 1 July 20X5. You are an audit supervisor of Olympus & Co, responsible for
the final audits of three clients for the year ended 31 May 20X5.
VerdantVision Co
VerdantVision Co (Verdant) specialises in manufacturing equipment which can
helps reduce toxic emissions in the production of chemicals.
Verdant has a material receivable balance owing from its customer, EcoRise
Co. Your team has reviewed the ageing of this balance and found that no
payments had been received from EcoRise for over six months, and Verdant
would not allow this balance to be circularised. Instead management has
assured your team that they will provide a written representation confirming
that the balance is recoverable.
Curly’s Current Co
Curly’s Current Co (CC) produces circuit boards which are sold to
manufacturers of electrical equipment such as computers and mobile phones.
One of the key materials used in production is copper wiring. In early May
20X5, production was halted for a week at the production facility as a number
of customers had returned goods, claiming faults in the circuit boards supplied.
On inspection, it was found that the copper used in the circuit boards was
corroded and therefore unsuitable for use.
CC’s business insurance covers the company for specific occasions when
business is interrupted. The finance director has informed you that a claim has
been made on 28 May 20X5 for $5 million which she has estimated to cover
the period when production was halted due to the problem with the corroded
copper. An adjustment has been made to recognise the $5 million as a
receivable as at 31 May 20X5.
Stark Foods Co
Stark Foods Co operates a chain of food wholesalers across the country and
its year end was 31 May 20X5.The final audit is nearly complete. Revenue for
the year is $78 million and profit before taxation is $7·5 million. The following
events has occurred subsequent to the year end.
A key supplier of Stark Foods is suing them for breach of contract. The lawsuit
was filed prior to the year end, and the sum claimed by them is $1 million. This
has been disclosed as a contingent liability in the notes to the financial
statements; however correspondence has just arrived from the supplier
indicating that they are willing to settle the case for a payment by Stark Foods
of $0·6 million. It is likely that the company will agree to this.
(a) With respect to the audit of VerdantVision Co,
(i) Discuss the appropriateness of written representations as a form of
audit evidence.
(ii) Describe additional audit procedures the auditor should now perform
in order to reach a conclusion on the balance to be included in the
financial statements. (6 marks)
(b) With respect to the audit of Curly’s Current Co, describe the audit
procedures to be performed in respect of the insurance claim.
(6 marks)
(c) With respect to the audit of Stark Foods Co,
(i) Discuss whether the financial statements require amendment;
(ii) Describe audit procedures that should be performed in order to form
a conclusion on the amendment; and
(iii) Explain the impact on the audit report should the issue remain
unresolved.
(8 marks)
(20 marks)"]

stem_zh:
1.Answer: Arrange for an independent professional accountant who is not a member of
the firm to review the fifth year’s audit work before issuing the audit opinion
Continuing the engagement without addressing the high fee dependency does not
mitigate the self-interest threat. The guidelines require measures to address the
dependency to avoid compromising the audit’s objectivity and independence.
Ceasing to be the auditor is premature. The firm should first evaluate whether an
independent review of the audit work can reduce the threats to an acceptable level.
Ceasing the audit engagement is a last resort, not the first step.
Requesting additional fees does not address the ethical threats of fee dependency. The
issue is not about the amount of fees but rather about the proportion of fees contributing
to the firm's total revenue and the impact on independence.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#1

stem_zh:
2.Answer:  The audit engagement partner should be removed from the audit team
If the engagement partner’s son accepts the role and obtains shares in the company, it
would constitute a self-interest threat but as the partner has already exceeded the
seven-year relationship rule in line with ACCA’s Code of Ethics and Conduct, the partner
should be rotated off the audit irrespective of the decision made by her son.
As Shinra is a listed company, an engagement quality control reviewer should already
be in place.
It is unlikely that the firm needs to resign from the audit (due to stated circumstances) as
the threats to objectivity can be mitigated.
Therefore, the first option is correct.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#2

stem_zh:
3.Answer: No safeguards can be applied and this basis for fee determination should be
rejected
The proposal in relation to the fees is a contingent fee basis which is expressly
prohibited by ACCA’s Code of Ethics and Conduct and therefore no safeguards will be
adequate to reduce the threat. The statement that the fee basis must be rejected is true.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#3

stem_zh:
4.Answer: Option C
Routine maintenance of payroll records. Providing accounting and bookkeeping
services to an audit client creates a self-review threat when there is a risk that the
results of the services will affect the accounting records or the financial statements on
which the firm will express an opinion.
Assistance with the selection of a new financial controller including the checking of
references is an example of recruitment services. Providing recruiting services to an
audit client might create a self interest, familiarity or intimidation threat.
Tax services whereby ClearView & Co would liaise with the tax authority on Arcadia
Co’s behalf. This gives rise to an advocacy threat as the firm would be acting as an
advocate on behalf of the client in front of a third party (tax authorities)
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#4

stem_zh:
5.Answer:
The audit committee’s terms of reference should include
reviewing and monitoring the external auditor’s
independence and objectivity
TRUE
The audit committee’s terms of reference should include
appointing and removing external auditors
FALSE
The audit committee’s terms of reference should include
evaluating and monitoring the effectiveness of the internal
audit function.
TRUE
Statement 2 is incorrect as the audit committee can recommend the appointment and
removal of external auditors. The final decision comes from the shareholders.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#5

6.Answer: Option C.
Inventory should be valued at the lower of cost and net realisable value. If it is valued at
$120 per barrel instead of $15 per barrel, inventory(assets) and profit will be overstated.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#6

stem_zh:
7.Answer: Option A
Procedures 1 and 3 can be conducted to verify the valuation assertion.
Procedure 2 is a test of control, testing the ‘system’ used to record receivables and
revenue.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#7

stem_zh:
8.Answer: The difference in relation to Energo Co represents a timing difference and
should be agreed to a pre-year end invoice
The first option is incorrect as the balance with Mizu Co would need to be followed up
due to the dispute.
The third option is incorrect as this represents a payment in transit and the payment would
need to be agreed to post year-end bank statements – if the cash was received pre year
end this would represent a cut -off issue as this should no longer be included in
receivables.
The fourth option is incorrect as the sample chosen should be verified even if there is no
response. The auditor should adopt alternative procedures.
Therefore, the second option is the only statement which is true as this does represent a
timing difference (invoice in transit) and should be agreed to a pre-year-end invoice.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#8

stem_zh:
9.Answer: Carrying out a positive circularisation of receivable balances in respect of the
year-end balances
Whilst a circularisation of trade receivable balances is intended to provide assurance
with regard to several audit objectives, the principal objective is to provide assurance
with regard to the existence. As the results from a positive circularisation are generally
recognised to be more reliable than those from a negative circularisation, the correct
answer is the first option.
Testing for existence by agreeing to sales documentation does not take account of
payments that may have been received.
The last option is a test of control.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#9

stem_zh:
10.Answer: 2 and 3
Statement 1 is incorrect as it is a test of control and cannot confirm the loan balance
Statement 4 is incorrect as this is a new loan so there will be no opening balance.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#10

stem_zh:
11. Answer:
The basis for opinion and opinion paragraphs should not
be combined and the basis for opinion paragraph should
be placed before the opinion paragraph.
FALSE
The title and the addressee in the extract of the draft
audit report are correct.
FALSE
If an emphasis of matter paragraph is required to be
added in an audit report, it will be placed before the
basis for opinion paragraph to provide appropriate
context to the auditor’s opinion.
FALSE
Statement 1 is incorrect as the first section of the auditor's report is the auditor's
opinion, directly followed by the basis for opinion section. This means that the basis for
opinion paragraph should be after the opinion paragraph.
Statement 2 is incorrect as the addressee should be the shareholders of Aether Drills
Co, not the directors.
Statement 3 is incorrect as although the placement of an Emphasis of Matter paragraph
in the auditor’s report depends on the nature of the information to be communicated and
the auditor’s judgment as to the relative significance of such information to intended
users, it may be placed immediately after the Basis of Opinion section but never before
it.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#11

stem_zh:
12.Answer: 1, 2 and 3
The paragraph states the materiality used during the audit. Although auditors include a
paragraph outlining the auditor’s responsibility including a description of the scope of
the audit, there is no requirement to specifically state the materiality level which has
been applied. This should be removed from the draft auditor’s report.
The paragraph states that audit procedures have ‘proven conclusively’ in respect of
trade receivables. This term is misleading, implying that every transaction has been
tested. Audit procedures provide a reasonable, but not absolute, level of assurance on
the financial statements, and conclusive proof is not an appropriate term to be used in
the auditor’s report.
The paragraph uses unprofessional wording by naming the finance director. The
auditor’s report should refer to management collectively and not single out one person
as being responsible for the financial statements.
In addition, it should not state that she ‘refused’ to make an adjustment.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#12

stem_zh:
13.Answer: A qualified ‘except for’ opinion
The scenario states that the matters given have been discussed with management and
will not be adjusted in the financial statements The trade receivables balance is material
at $2·5 million, which is in excess of the materiality threshold of $0.82 million used in the
audit and the opinion will be qualified due to material a misstatement Therefore the third
option is correct.
The auditor’s report uses a disclaimer of opinion, which is used when the auditor cannot
form an opinion, usually due to lack of audit evidence, which does not appear to be the
case here. Therefore, the first option is incorrect.
If the auditor considers it necessary to communicate a matter other than those that are
presented or disclosed in the financial statements that, in the auditor’s judgment, is
relevant to users’ understanding of the audit, the auditor’s responsibilities or the
auditor’s report, the auditor shall include an Other Matter paragraph in the auditor’s
report. This is not relevant here or to the receivables balance. Therefore, the second
option is incorrect.
The matter is material but is unlikely to be pervasive to the financial statements based
on the level of profit of $11 million. Therefore, a qualified opinion is sufficient, and an
adverse opinion will not be given. The fourth option is incorrect.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#13

stem_zh:
14.Answer: 1, 2 and 3
An Emphasis of Matter paragraph is used to refer to a matter appropriately presented or
disclosed in the financial statements which, in the auditor’s judgement, is of such
importance that it is fundamental to users’ understanding of the financial statements.
The court case and its potential legal consequences are not material, being well below
the materiality threshold of $0.82 million.
The matter is not fundamental to users’ understanding of the financial statements. Due
to the immaterial nature of the matter, it need not be referred to in the auditor’s report at
all.
The auditor has reached the conclusion that the court case has not been accounted for
correctly.
The Emphasis of Matter paragraph should only be used to highlight matters which have
been appropriately accounted for and disclosed within the financial statements, and its
use to describe non-compliance with the relevant financial reporting standard is not
appropriate.
source_doc: ACCA:aa:objective:AA  24年12月考季 官方Pre-Dec Mock A.pdf#14

stem_zh:
15.Answer: 3 and 4 only
Statement 1 is false as not all subsequent events will require an adjustment to the
numbers within the financial statements. IAS 10 Events after the Reporting Period
makes a distinction between an adjusting and non-adjusting event. Only material
adjusting events would require an amendment to the figures within the financial
statements.
Statement 2 is false as while a non-adjusting event would not require a change to the
numbers within the financial statements, IAS 10 may require a disclosure to be made. If
the non-adjusting event is material, non-disclosure could still result in a modification to
the audit report.
Statement 3 is true as the auditor is required to carry out procedures up to the date of
the auditor’s report to gain sufficient appropriate audit evidence that all relevant
subsequent events have been identified and dealt with appropriately. After the auditor’s
report is issued, the auditor does not need to actively look for subsequent events but is
only required to respond to subsequent events which they become aware of.
Statement 4 is true as ISA 560 Subsequent Events requires the auditor to obtain written
confirmation from management/those charged with governance that all subsequent
events have been identified and dealt with in accordance with the appropriate reporting
framework.
Suggested solution 16 (part (a))
Audit risk Auditor's response
This is the first year Oogway & Co is
carrying out the audit of Lotus Co.
As a result, there will be a lack of
knowledge regarding
the company's accounting policies and
transactions which could lead to a
misinterpretation of audit evidence or the

This will increase detection risk and
specifically
non-sampling risk. There is also less
assurance over opening balances as
Oogway & Co did not perform the audit
last year.
Allocate additional time in the audit

Ensure a comprehensive planning

this information to be shared and allocate
more experienced staff than usual to the
audit.
Increase audit procedures over opening
balances.

There is a risk that inventory is
overvalued due to the inclusion of

If the adjustments are not made

The audit team should increase the

Lotus Co receives an annual trade

Co, and a trade discounts receivable of

The receivable should only be recognised

The directors have extended the useful

However, there is a risk that this reduction

Discuss with the directors the rationale for

Also, the five-year life should be
compared to how often these assets are

The directors need to reach a profit
before tax of $1.5 million in order to

There is a risk that the directors will try to

and estimates.
The audit team should be aware of the
increased risks of manipulation and
should assign more experienced audit

judgemental areas.
Also, adequate time should be allocated
for team members to obtain an

The team needs to maintain professional

The financial controller of Lotus was

If it is probable that Lotus will make

If the payment is possible rather than

Suggested solution 17 (part (b))
Substantive procedures for directors’ bonuses
• Obtain a schedule of the directors’ bonus and cast the schedule to ensure its
accuracy. Agree the amount to that disclosed in the financial statements.
• Review the schedule of current liabilities and confirm the bonus accrual is
included as a year-end liability.
• Agree the individual bonus payments from the schedule to the post year-end
payroll records.
• Recalculate the bonus payments and agree the criteria to supporting
documentation and the percentage rates to be paid to the directors’ service
contracts.
• Confirm the amount of each bonus paid by agreeing to the post year-end cash
book and bank statements.
• Compare the profit before tax used in the bonus calculation to the final profit
before tax figure to confirm whether any adjustment is required to the bonus paid
and discuss any differences with management.
• Agree the amounts paid to each director to board minutes and contracts to
ensure the amounts included in the current year financial statements are fully
accrued and disclosed.
• Review the board minutes to identify whether any additional payments relating to
this year have been agreed for any directors.
• Obtain a written representation from management confirming the completeness
of directors’ remuneration including the bonus.
• Review the disclosures made regarding the bonus paid to directors and assess
whether these are in compliance with local legislation.
Suggested solution 18 (part (c))
Quality management deficiencies
There has been a breach of relevant law and regulations. The audit team should have
considered the requirements of ISA 250 Consideration of Laws and Regulations in an
Audit of Financial Statements.
The auditor is required by ISA 315 to gain an understanding of the legal and regulatory
framework in which the audited entity operates. Therefore, the auditor should have
ensured that a full knowledge and understanding of the relevant data protection laws
and regulations was obtained in order to evaluate the implications of non-compliance.
ISA 250 requires that when non-compliance is identified or suspected, the auditor shall
obtain an understanding of the nature of the act and the circumstances in which it has
occurred, and further information to evaluate the possible effect on the financial
statements.
Therefore, procedures should have been performed to obtain evidence about the
suspected non-compliance, for example, to discuss the breach with management to
understand how it happened, whether it was due to deliberate action or an unintentional
mistake, and who was responsible.
In addition, the audit team should have performed further procedures, for example,
discussion with the company’s legal advisers to understand the legal consequences of
the breach.
From the information provided, it seems that the audit team failed to obtain more
information or evidence due to their belief that the situation had little to do with the audit.
The audit has therefore not been performed appropriately.
The audit engagement partner is required to review the audit documentation to be
satisfied that audit work is complete, and that sufficient appropriate audit evidence has
been obtained to support the conclusions reached and for the auditor’s report to be
issued.
There are also quality issues with the selection of samples to be used in tests of detail.
First, the use of judgemental sampling may result in sample sizes which are smaller
than would have been selected using statistical sampling methods, or in the selection of
items which are not representative of the whole population.
The auditor is required to determine a sample size sufficient to reduce sampling risk to
an acceptably low level, and to select items for the sample in such a way that each
sampling unit in the population has a chance of selection. The risk is that the use of
judgement has led to inappropriate audit conclusions being made.
It seems that some items in the populations were completely excluded from the sample.
There is a high risk that these items have not been subject to sufficient audit procedures
and that the relevant assertions have not been covered by audit testing. For example, if
the non-current assets have not been physically verified, and no other procedures
relevant to their existence have been performed, then assets recognised in the financial
statements may be overstated.
Suggested solution 19 (part (d))
Importance of communicating with those charged with governance
It is important for the auditors to report to those charged with governance as it helps in
the following ways:
• It assists the auditor and those charged with governance in understanding
matters related to the audit, and in developing a constructive working
relationship. This relationship is developed while maintaining the auditor’s
independence and objectivity.
• It helps the auditor in obtaining from those charged with governance, information
relevant to the audit. For example, those charged with governance may assist
the auditor in understanding the entity and its environment, in identifying
appropriate sources of audit evidence and in providing information about specific
transactions or events.
• It helps those charged with governance in fulfilling their responsibility to oversee
the financial reporting process, thereby reducing the risks of material
misstatement of the financial statements.
• It promotes effective two-way communication between the auditor and those
charged with governance.
Matters to be communicated to those charged with governance
• The auditor’s responsibilities with regards to providing an opinion on the
financial statements and that they have carried out their work in accordance
with International Standards on Auditing.
• The auditor should explain the planned approach to the audit as well as the
audit timetable.
• Any key audit risks identified during the planning stage should be
communicated.
• Any significant difficulties encountered during the audit should be
communicated.
• Significant matters arising during the audit, as well as significant accounting
adjustments.
• During the audit, any significant deficiencies in the internal control system
identified should be communicated in writing or verbally.
• How the external auditor and internal auditor may work together and any
planned use of the work of the internal audit function.
• Those charged with governance should be notified of any written
representations required by the auditor.
• If any suspected frauds are identified during the audit, these must be
communicated.
• If the auditors are intending to make any modifications to the audit opinion,
these should be communicated to those charged with governance.
• For listed entities, a confirmation that the auditors have complied with ethical
standards and appropriate safeguards have been put in place for any ethical
threats identified.
Suggested solution 20 (part (a))
Significant deficiencies
Examples of matters the external auditor may consider in determining whether a
deficiency in internal controls is significant include:
− The likelihood of the deficiencies leading to material misstatements in the
financial statements in the future.
− The susceptibility to loss or fraud of the related asset or liability.
− The subjectivity and complexity of determining estimated amounts.
− The financial statement amounts exposed to the deficiencies.
− The volume of activity that has occurred or could occur in the account
balance or class of transactions exposed to the deficiency or deficiencies.
− The importance of the controls to the financial reporting process.
− The cause and frequency of the exceptions detected as a result of the
deficiencies in the controls.
Suggested solution 21 (part (b))
Control deficiency Control recommendation Test of control
The finance director
authorises the monthly
supplier payments listing,
but only views the total
amount of payments to be
made.
Without looking at the
detail of the payments list,
as well as supporting
documentation, there is a
risk that suppliers could be
being paid an incorrect
amount, or that sums are
being paid to fictitious
suppliers.
The finance director should
review the whole payments

As part of this, the finance

any duplicates or any

should be evidenced by

payments listing
A sample of payment

prior to the bank transfer

There is no requirement for

This could result in sundry

Suggested solution 22 (part (c))
Advantages and disadvantages of potential non-executive directors (NEDs)
Advantages
• Shimmer Pane already has experience of being a NED for another company and
she has sat on an audit committee, hence she will be familiar with what the role
entails and will be able to bring experience of being a NED to VistaCraft.
• In addition, Shimmer Pane has indicated she is agreeable to being paid a fixed
fee which is not profit related; this is important as an independent NED’s
remuneration should be unrelated to the performance of the company.
• David Shine is currently a finance director and so he possesses recent and
relevant financial experience which is required for at least one member of the
audit committee.
• In addition, he operates in the manufacturing industry and so would be aware of
key issues facing companies like VistaCraft and so would have an appropriate
mix of experience and knowledge.
Disadvantages
• Appointing Shimmer Pane as a NED has disadvantages as she works for a
banking company and so would not have relevant experience of companies such
as VistaCraft; hence she could lack the critical skills and relevant experience
needed to provide meaningful advice to the executive directors.
• In addition, Shimmer Pane is already an executive director for a large
multinational company and a NED for another company; it might be difficult for
her to devote sufficient time to her role at VistaCraft.
• David Shine is the brother of the chief executive and therefore he is not an
independent NED. He might be inclined to agree with the chief executive as he is
his brother rather than providing the level of objective judgement required from a
NED.
• Also, he wants a contract as a NED for a period of seven years; all directors
including NEDs must be subject to re-election at regular intervals not exceeding
three years.
Suggested solution 23 - 25
(a) Substantive procedures for bank reconciliation
• Obtain Arctic’s bank account reconciliation and cast to check the additions to
ensure arithmetical accuracy.
• Agree the balance per the bank reconciliation to an original year-end bank
statement and to the bank confirmation letter.
• Agree the reconciliation’s balance per the cash book to the year-end cash book.
• Trace all the outstanding lodgements to the pre-year-end cash book, post year-
end bank statement and also to paying-in-book pre year end.
• Trace all unpresented cheques through to a pre-year-end cash book and post
year-end statement.
• For any unusual amounts or significant delays, obtain explanations from
management.
• Examine any old unpresented cheques to assess if they need to be written back
into the purchase ledger as they are no longer valid to be presented.
(b) Substantive procedures for revaluation property, plant and equipment (PPE)
• Obtain a schedule of all PPE revalued during the year and cast to confirm
completeness and accuracy of the revaluation adjustment and agree to trial
balance and financial statements.
• Consider the competence and capability of the valuer by assessing through
enquiry his qualification, membership of a professional body and experience in
valuing these types of assets.
• Consider whether the valuation undertaken provides sufficiently objective audit
evidence. Discuss with management whether Henry has any financial interest in
Arctic Co which along with the family relationship could have had an impact on
his independence.
• Agree the revalued amounts to the valuation statement provided by the valuer.
• Consider if all assets in the same category have been revalued in line with IAS
16 Property, Plant and Equipment.
• Agree the revalued amounts for these assets are included correctly in the non-
current assets register.
• Recalculate the total revaluation adjustment and agree correctly recorded in the
revaluation surplus.
• Recalculate the depreciation charge for the year to ensure that for the assets
revalued during the year, the depreciation was based on the correct valuation
and was for 12 months.
• Review the financial statements disclosures relating to the revaluation to ensure
they comply with IAS 16.
(c) Substantive procedures for completeness of trade payables and accruals
• Compare the total trade payables and list of accruals against prior year and
investigate any significant differences. This can be done using the audit software.
• Select a sample of post year-end payments from the bank ledger account; if they
relate to the current year, follow through to the list of individual suppliers or
accruals listing to ensure they are recorded in the correct period.
• Obtain supplier statements and reconcile these to the the list of individual
suppliers and investigate any reconciling items.
•  Select a sample of payable balances and perform a trade payables’
circularisation, follow up any non-replies and any reconciling items between the
balance confirmed and the trade payables’ balance.
• Review after date invoices and goods returned notes to ensure no further items
need to be accrued.
• Enquire of management their process for identifying goods received but not
invoiced or logged in the list of individual suppliers and ensure that it is
reasonable to ensure completeness of payables.
• Audit software can be utilised to recalculate the accruals for goods received not
invoiced at the year end.
• Audit software can be used to undertake cut-off testing by assessing whether the
dates of the last goods received notes recorded relate to pre year end; and that
any with a date of 1 June 20X5 onwards were excluded from trade payables.
Suggested solution 26 (part (d))
Audit report
The information regarding Ice Co’s cash show difficulties was received after the year
end but provided further evidence of the recoverability of the receivable balance at the
year end. If Ice Co was experiencing cash flow difficulties just a few months after the
year end, it is highly unlikely that $692,000 was recoverable as at 31 May 20X5 and
therefore this is an adjusting event.
The directors should amend the 20X5 financial statements by making an allowance for
receivables or by writing off the receivables balance.
The receivables balance and profit are overstated.
$692,000 is material as it is 7.8% of profit before tax and 1.1% of total assets.
A modified auditor's opinion would be required. As the misstatement is material but not
pervasive, a qualified opinion would be necessary.
A basis for qualified opinion would be included in the auditor's report explaining the
issue.
The heading of the opinion would change to ‘Qualified opinion’ and would state that
'except for' this matter the financial statements give a true and fair view.
Marking guide 16 – 19
Lotus
(a)  Audit risks and auditor’s responses : 1 mark for audit risk, 1 mark for auditor’s
responses up to a maximum of 16 marks
(b)  Substantive procedures for directors’ bonuses: 1 mark per well-described procedure
up to a maximum of 5 marks.
(c)  1 mark for each well explained quality management deficiency up to a maximum of
5 marks
(d)  1 mark each for a valid discussion of importance of communication up to a maximum
of 2 marks. 1 mark each for examples of matters to be communicated up to a
maximum of 2 marks.
Marking guide 20-22
VistaCraft
(a)  1 mark each for example up to a maximum of 3 marks.
(b)  1 mark each for well explained deficiency, 1 mark for each relevant
recommendation, 1 mark for each relevant test of control, up to a maximum of 12
marks
(c)  Up to 1 mark per well explained point up to a maximum of 5 marks.
Advantages
• Shimmer Pane has experience as a NED and of audit committee role.
• Shimmer Pane has agreed to a non-profit related fee.
• David Shine has relevant and recent financial experience as he is a finance
director.
• David Shine operates in the manufacturing industry.
Disadvantages
• Shimmer Pane could lack critical skills and relevant experience of the
manufacturing industry.
• Shimmer Pane may not have sufficient time as she has a number of roles
already.
• David Shine is the brother of the chief executive and so is not independent.
• David Shine wants a seven-year fixed contract which is contrary to corporate
governance principles.
Marking guide 23-25
Arctic
(a)  Substantive procedures bank reconciliation: 1 mark per well -described procedure,
up to a maximum of 5
(b)  Substantive procedures for PPE: 1 mark per well -described procedure, up to a
maximum of 5
(c) Substantive procedures for trade payables and accruals:1 mark per well-described
procedure, up to a maximum of 5

The following scenario relates to questions 6-10
It is 1 July 20X5. You are an audit supervisor of AeroSho & Co and you are involved in
the audit of Hikari FuelTech Co for the year ended 31 May 20X5. Hikari FuelTech Co
(Hikari) sells petrol, aviation fuel and similar oil-based products. One of its key customers
is the government of the country it is based in. The audit work for this year is complete,
apart from the matters referred to below.
Inventory
As part of Hikari ’s service contract with the government, it is required to hold an
emergency inventory reserve of 6,000 barrels of aviation fuel. The inventory is to be used
if the supply of aviation fuel is interrupted due to unforeseen events such as natural
disaster or terrorist activity.
This fuel has in the past been valued at its cost price of $15 a barrel. The current value
of aviation fuel is $120 a barrel. Although the audit work is complete, the finance director
of Hikari has now decided to show the ‘real’ value of this closing inventory in the financial
statements by valuing closing inventory of fuel at market value.
Trade receivables
The trade receivables balance has risen significantly and is now a material balance.

team on balances as at 31 May 20X5 are detailed below.

It is 1 July 20X5. You are an audit manager in Titanis & Co, responsible for the audit of

show profit before tax of $11 million.
The audit for the year end is nearing completion, and several matters have been

The matters have been discussed with management who have confirmed that these will
not be adjusted in the financial statements:

2. A court case began in January 20X5 involving an ex-employee who is suing Aether
for unfair dismissal. Lawyers estimate that damages of $15,000 are probable to be paid.
The financial statements include a note describing the court case and quantifying the
potential damages, but no adjustment has been made to include it in the financial
statements.
Sakura has produced a draft audit report for your review, an extract of which is shown
below:
Independent auditor’s report
To the directors of Aether Drills Co
Basis for opinion and disclaimer of opinion
We have performed our audit based on a materiality level of $0.82 million. Our audit
procedures have proven conclusively that trade receivables are materially misstated.
The finance director of Aether Drills Co, Kurofune Khan, has refused to make an
adjustment to write off a significant trade receivables balance. In our opinion, the
financial statements of Aether Drills Co are materially misstated, and we therefore
express a disclaimer of opinion because we do not think they are fairly presented.
Emphasis of Matter paragraph
Aether Drills Co is facing a legal claim for an amount of $15,000 from an ex-employee.
In our opinion this amount should be recognised as a provision, but it is not included in
the statement of financial position. We draw your attention to this breach of the relevant
IFRS.
